Phase-31.step-4: Replay one bounded voice attempt on the chosen app variant
Phase-31.step-5: Replay one bounded video attempt on the chosen app variant
Phase-31.step-6: Compare app-variant media packets against the completed Phase-29 and Phase-30 packets
Phase-31.step-7: Assemble the bounded app-variant handoff, capture, media, and comparison packets
Phase-31.step-8: Issue the bounded app-variant decision for the tested Telegram setup
Phase-31.note-1: Phase-31 starts only after the completed Phase-30 no-go packet, which already preserved the green transport baseline and showed that a topology swap alone does not change the Telegram no-go class.
Phase-31.note-2: The new phase must keep one Telegram-specific app variant distinct from earlier topology-only packets so a later comparison can say whether the app variant changed the no-go class.
Phase-31.note-3: The chosen Phase-31 variant keeps the completed Phase-30 truly local `n0wss-client` handoff fixed and changes one Telegram-specific dimension only: if the tested build exposes a calls-proxy toggle, keep proxying messages and files through `127.0.0.1:1080` while disabling proxy usage for calls only.
Phase-31.note-4: On the tested Desktop build that variant was unavailable because the app exposes no separate calls-proxy toggle, so the remaining Phase-31 tail is superseded rather than silently replaced by another app variable.

Phase-32.name: Telegram Mobile Calls-Proxy Variant
Phase-32.goal: Evaluate one bounded Telegram Mobile workaround variant above the preserved green transport baseline and compare it directly against the completed Desktop no-go packets.
Phase-32.step-1: Freeze the mobile-only workaround boundary after the blocked Desktop variant
Phase-32.step-2: Explicitly supersede the blocked Phase-31 tail
Phase-32.step-3: Freeze the exact mobile handoff profile with explicit calls-proxy toggle state
Phase-32.step-4: Define the bounded capture packet for the mobile wave
Phase-32.step-5: Replay one bounded mobile voice attempt
Phase-32.step-6: Replay one bounded mobile video attempt
Phase-32.step-7: Compare the mobile packets against the completed Desktop packets
Phase-32.step-8: Assemble the bounded mobile handoff, capture, media, and comparison packets
Phase-32.step-9: Issue the bounded mobile decision for the tested Telegram setup
Phase-32.note-1: Phase-32 starts only after the blocked Phase-31 packet, which already proved that the chosen Desktop app variant is unavailable on the tested build.
Phase-32.note-2: The new phase must keep the mobile setup distinct from the completed Desktop packets so a later comparison can say whether the mobile app behavior changed the no-go class.
Phase-32.note-3: The chosen mobile variant keeps the same remote tunnel path but replaces the Desktop-only localhost handoff with one dedicated LAN-facing mobile listener on a separate port.
Phase-32.note-4: The already-working local Desktop listener on `127.0.0.1:1080` must remain untouched during the mobile wave so text messages, media files, and large files keep their preserved path.
Phase-32.note-5: On 2026-03-30 the tested Android packet used `SOCKS5 192.168.31.241:11080` with `Use proxy for calls = enabled`, connected successfully, but text messages became high-latency, media files no longer sent or received, and both voice and video stalled at `Обмен ключами шифрования`.
Phase-32.note-6: The bounded comparison against the completed Desktop packets changed the no-go class for neither voice nor video and degraded the ordinary app path instead of helping it, so Phase-32 closes as no-go for the tested Android variant.

Phase-33.name: Telegram Terminal Decision
Phase-33.goal: Decide whether Telegram workaround exploration should stop for the tested variants or whether exactly one genuinely new branch remains.
Phase-33.step-1: Freeze the bounded terminal hypothesis after the completed Desktop and Android no-go packets
Phase-33.step-2: Assemble the exact tested-variant matrix across the completed Telegram waves
Phase-33.step-3: Screen the remaining Telegram-specific branch space for genuine novelty
Phase-33.step-4: Freeze explicit stop criteria for workaround exploration
Phase-33.step-5: Assemble the bounded final Telegram evidence packet
Phase-33.step-6: Issue the bounded final Telegram decision for the tested variants
Phase-33.step-7: Freeze the safe operator end-state after Telegram workaround exploration
Phase-33.note-1: Phase-33 starts only after the preserved green transport baseline and the completed Desktop and Android no-go packets are all simultaneously visible.
Phase-33.note-2: The phase must not reopen generic transport diagnosis or any already-spent Telegram workaround branch.
Phase-33.note-3: The phase may either stop Telegram workaround exploration for the tested variants or justify exactly one still-unspent narrow branch, but not both.
Phase-33.note-4: On 2026-03-30 the tested-variant matrix was frozen explicitly: Phase-29 preserved the SSH-forwarded Desktop no-go packet, Phase-30 preserved the truly local Desktop no-go packet, Phase-31 preserved the unavailable Desktop-only variant packet, and Phase-32 preserved the Android mobile calls-proxy no-go packet.
Phase-33.note-5: The bounded branch-screen result on the same date exhausted the remaining Telegram-specific branch space for the tested variants because any further Desktop or Android rerun would only replay an already-spent branch without a genuinely new bounded variable.
Phase-33.note-6: The explicit stop criteria now forbid reopening the SSH-forwarded Desktop route, the truly local Desktop route, the unavailable Desktop-only calls-proxy toggle, or the tested Android `Use proxy for calls = enabled` route as if they were new work.
Phase-33.note-7: The bounded final decision for Phase-33 is to stop workaround exploration as no-go for the tested Telegram Desktop and tested Android variants while keeping the green transport baseline intact and without widening the claim beyond those tested variants.
Phase-33.note-8: The operator cleanup end-state preserves only the working Desktop listener at `127.0.0.1:1080`, removes temporary Telegram-only mobile listener surfaces from the baseline, and preserves the already-working Desktop path for text messages, media files, and large files.

Phase-34.name: Telegram Media Path Attribution
Phase-34.goal: Attribute Telegram voice and video media behavior after key exchange on the already-working Desktop baseline without reopening spent workaround branches.
Phase-34.step-1: Freeze the bounded Telegram media-path attribution question above the preserved green baseline
Phase-34.step-2: Freeze one exact Desktop attribution capture topology without changing the working baseline
Phase-34.step-3: Define the bounded Desktop network-capture surface for media attribution
Phase-34.step-4: Freeze the governed server-side correlation contract for attribution waves
Phase-34.step-5: Replay one bounded Desktop voice attribution wave
Phase-34.step-6: Replay one bounded Desktop video attribution wave without inheriting voice
Phase-34.step-7: Classify voice and video packets as governed-media, direct-media, signaling-only, or insufficient evidence
Phase-34.step-8: Issue the bounded next-step decision after media-path attribution
Phase-34.note-1: Phase-34 starts only after the preserved working Desktop baseline, the green transport baseline, and the completed terminal Telegram no-go matrix are all visible together.
Phase-34.note-2: The phase must not reopen generic transport diagnosis or any already-spent Desktop or Android workaround branch.
Phase-34.note-3: The only approved new question is attribution: whether Telegram media attempts the governed SOCKS path, a direct path outside SOCKS, or no real media path at all after key exchange.
Phase-34.note-4: The already-working Desktop path for text messages, media files, and large files at `127.0.0.1:1080` must remain untouched during this attribution wave.
Phase-34.note-5: On 2026-03-30 the bounded Desktop voice-attribution packet progressed through request, ringing, and key exchange before dropping; loopback and uplink captures stayed active on the preserved SOCKS and WSS surfaces, but broader workstation capture also recorded fresh UDP egress to Telegram-owned `91.108.*` addresses on ports `598`, `599`, and `1400` while the same bounded server-side correlation packet stayed limited to fresh WSS handshakes without fresh datagram relay markers.
Phase-34.note-6: On the same date the bounded Desktop video-attribution packet progressed through request, ringing, key exchange, connection error, and drop; broader workstation capture again recorded fresh direct traffic to Telegram infrastructure while the same bounded server-side correlation packet again stayed limited to fresh WSS handshakes without fresh datagram relay markers.
Phase-34.note-7: The bounded Phase-34 classifier now converges for both voice and video on `direct-media outside SOCKS`.
Phase-34.note-8: The bounded Phase-34 decision is therefore not another transport repair: the next branch, if any, must be Telegram-specific/app-behavior work around direct media outside the governed SOCKS envelope.

Phase-35.name: Telegram Forced Media Topology
Phase-35.goal: Test one exact forced-routing topology variant for Telegram calls without changing the normal working Desktop baseline and determine whether the direct-media class changes.
Phase-35.step-1: Freeze the new topology-only branch above the Phase-34 attribution verdict
Phase-35.step-2: Freeze one exact forced-routing topology profile without changing the normal Desktop baseline
Phase-35.step-3: Define the isolated Telegram operator runbook for the forced topology wave
Phase-35.step-4: Prepare the bounded forced-topology capture packet
Phase-35.step-5: Run one bounded forced-topology Desktop voice wave
Phase-35.step-6: Run one bounded forced-topology Desktop video wave without inheriting voice
Phase-35.step-7: Compare forced-topology packets against the Phase-34 attribution verdict
Phase-35.note-1: Phase-35 starts only after the preserved working Desktop baseline and the Phase-34 `direct-media outside SOCKS` verdict are both visible together.
Phase-35.note-2: The phase must not reopen generic transport diagnosis or replay any spent SOCKS-only Telegram branch as if it were new work.
Phase-35.note-3: The only approved new variable is topology class: whether forced routing can keep Telegram media inside the governed envelope.
Phase-35.note-4: The normal Desktop path for text messages, media files, and large files at `127.0.0.1:1080` must remain untouched outside the isolated forced-topology runbook.
Phase-35.note-5: On 2026-03-30 the first forced-topology execution blocker was narrowed to the snap Telegram Desktop launcher boundary: `ip netns exec` broke the snap tracking-cgroup/runtime surface, while `systemd-run` with `NetworkNamespacePath` preserved the host mount and cgroup context and still placed the tested Telegram process inside the dedicated network namespace.

Phase-36.name: Telegram Transparent Forced Routing
Phase-36.goal: Test one exact transparent-routing topology that preserves the normal Desktop baseline while proving interception, governed handoff, and changed Telegram media attribution if any.
Phase-36.step-1: Freeze the transparent-routing-only branch above the Phase-34 verdict and the superseded Phase-35 packet
Phase-36.step-2: Explicitly supersede the old Phase-35 tail as a contract mismatch
Phase-36.step-3: Freeze one exact transparent-routing profile without changing the normal Desktop baseline
Phase-36.step-5: Prepare the bounded transparent-routing capture packet
Phase-36.step-6: Run one bounded transparent-routing Desktop voice wave
Phase-36.step-7: Run one bounded transparent-routing Desktop video wave without inheriting voice
Phase-36.step-8: Compare transparent-routing packets against the Phase-34 baseline and the superseded Phase-35 packet
Phase-36.note-1: Phase-36 starts only after the preserved working Desktop baseline, the Phase-34 `direct-media outside SOCKS` verdict, and the superseded Phase-35 contract-mismatch packet are all visible together.
Phase-36.note-2: The phase must not reopen generic transport diagnosis and must not reuse the old Phase-35 SOCKS-only containment packet as if it had already proven transparent interception.
Phase-36.note-3: The only approved new variable is routing class: whether transparent interception can force Telegram media into a governed local handoff surface before it escapes directly.
Phase-36.note-4: The normal Desktop path for text messages, media files, and large files at `127.0.0.1:1080` must remain untouched outside the isolated transparent-routing runbook.
Phase-36.note-5: Transparent-routing evidence is valid only if namespace proof, interception proof, governed handoff proof, workstation captures, and server correlation are all recorded inside the same bounded packet window.

Phase-37.name: Telegram Transparent Interception Helper
Phase-37.goal: Introduce one explicit helper-backed transparent interception branch so Phase-36 can become runnable without changing the preserved normal Desktop baseline.
Phase-37.step-1: Freeze the helper-only blocker boundary above the blocked Phase-36 packet
Phase-37.step-2: Choose one exact transparent interception helper class
Phase-37.step-3: Define the bounded install, setup, remove, and cleanup path for the helper
Phase-37.step-4: Freeze the exact helper-backed runtime topology
Phase-37.step-5: Prove the helper outside Telegram UI with synthetic TCP and UDP smoke
Phase-37.step-6: Run one bounded Telegram Desktop voice attempt through the helper-backed topology
Phase-37.step-7: Run one bounded Telegram Desktop video attempt through the same helper-backed topology without inheriting voice
Phase-37.step-8: Compare helper-backed packets against the Phase-34 direct-media baseline and the blocked Phase-36 packet
Phase-37.note-1: Phase-37 starts only after the blocked Phase-36 helper-gap packet is visible together with the preserved green transport baseline.
Phase-37.note-2: The phase must not reopen generic transport diagnosis and must not spend any new Telegram UI packet before helper smoke is green.
Phase-37.note-3: The only approved new variable is the transparent interception helper surface; the preserved normal Desktop baseline at `127.0.0.1:1080` must remain untouched.
Phase-37.note-4: Any package install or helper bootstrap must be explicit, reversible, experiment-local, and recorded in the operator runbook.
Phase-37.note-5: Voice and video claims remain split unless the helper-backed comparison packet proves both changed class.
Phase-37.note-6: On 2026-03-30 the helper technology decision fixed one exact helper class for the current host: Ubuntu package `redsocks` candidate `0.5-2build4`, chosen because it is already available on the workstation package archive, supports transparent TCP redirection plus UDP packets via upstream SOCKS5, and can remain experiment-local without changing the existing n0wss transport contract before synthetic helper smoke begins.
Phase-37.note-7: On the same date the bounded helper install runbook was proven on the workstation: `apt-get install -y redsocks` installed the helper and its `libevent` dependency, the package exposed `/usr/sbin/redsocks` and `/etc/redsocks.conf`, and the distro service was immediately stopped and disabled so no ambient helper daemon remained in the preserved Desktop baseline.
Phase-37.note-8: On the same date the helper runtime profile was frozen with an honest boundary: `redsocks` provides generic transparent TCP interception into the preserved host-local SOCKS5 surface at `127.0.0.1:1080`, while `redudp` remains bounded to one fixed synthetic destination tuple per section and therefore cannot yet justify a claim of arbitrary Telegram media UDP interception before helper smoke proves the fixed-destination packet.
Phase-37.note-9: During the first helper smoke on 2026-03-30 the host-side REDIRECT rule counted the synthetic namespace TCP packet but no helper session reached `redsocks`; the first divergent block was the helper listener bind surface, and the runtime profile was corrected to require helper listeners on the host-side namespace-facing address instead of `127.0.0.1` for redirected namespace packets.
Phase-37.note-10: After the helper listener-bind fix on 2026-03-30, bounded synthetic helper smoke turned green on both packet classes: transparent TCP reached the governed SOCKS/WSS path, and bounded UDP returned successfully when the helper targeted the deterministic server-side echo tuple `91.99.128.146:55123`; no Telegram UI packet was spent before that smoke turned green.

ExecutionPolicy.worker-owns: Only module source files and module-local tests inside the approved write scope.
